mnemonics.title: ALOHA - A Lofty Hypothesis Allows Acknowledgment
mnemonics.content: Pure ALOHA: Send whenever you want. If collision occurs, retransmit after random time. Vulnerable time = 2 × frame time. Throughput = 18.4% (0.184). Slotted ALOHA: Send only at start of time slots. Vulnerable time = frame time. Throughput = 36.8% (0.368). Remember: Slotted is exactly double the efficiency of Pure ALOHA.

mnemonics.title: CSMA Types - 'Persistent' Determines Response
mnemonics.content: 1-Persistent CSMA: Listen; if idle, send immediately (highest collision risk). Non-Persistent CSMA: Listen; if idle, send; if busy, wait random time then repeat. p-Persistent CSMA (for slotted channels): If idle, send with probability p; wait for next slot with probability (1-p). Remember: '1' = most aggressive, 'non' = most patient.

mnemonics.title: CSMA/CD - Collision Detection = 'Detect and Drop'
mnemonics.content: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection: Listen before sending. While sending, monitor for collisions. If collision detected, stop sending immediately, send jam signal, wait random backoff time before retransmitting. Used in Ethernet. Minimum frame size required = 512 bits (to ensure collision detection). Binary Exponential Backoff: Wait time = r × slot time, where r ∈ [0, 2^n - 1], n = number of collisions.

mnemonics.title: CSMA/CA - Avoids Before It Can Collide
mnemonics.content: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance: Used in wireless (Wi-Fi). Uses RTS/CTS (Request to Send / Clear to Send) and ACK (Acknowledgment) frames. NAV (Network Allocation Vector) - virtual carrier sense. Slot selection using binary exponential backoff. Collision window: DIFS (DCF Interframe Space) → Backoff → Data → ACK → SIFS.

mnemonics.title: Token Passing - 'Take Token To Reach Terminal'
mnemonics.content: Token Ring: Token circulates on a ring. Only holder can transmit. 3 frames: Token (free), Frame (data), Release (return token). Token is 3 bytes. Max token holding time controls data transmission. Token Bus: Token circulates on a logical bus (simulated ring). Both use Token Passing Protocol - efficient, no collisions, deterministic delay.

mnemonics.title: Channelization - 'Frequency Time Code Division'
mnemonics.content: FDMA (Frequency Division): Each station gets exclusive frequency band. TDMA (Time Division): Time slots allocated to stations. CDMA (Code Division): All stations use same frequency but unique codes (spreading codes) to distinguish. CDMA = Frequency shared, Time shared, Code separated. More complex but robust.

acronyms.usage: Layer 2 protocol sublayer managing access to shared transmission medium
Original random access protocol developed at University of Hawaii
Protocol where stations listen to channel before transmitting

acronyms.expansion: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
acronyms.usage: CSMA variant for wired networks (Ethernet) that detects and handles collisions

acronyms.expansion: Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Avoidance
acronyms.usage: CSMA variant for wireless networks (Wi-Fi) that tries to prevent collisions

acronyms.expansion: Request to Send / Clear to Send
acronyms.usage: Handshake mechanism in CSMA/CA to avoid hidden node problem
Virtual carrier sense in wireless - indicates how long channel will be busy
Wait time before data transmission in Wi-Fi DCF
Short wait time for ACK, CTS responses in Wi-Fi
Channelization method dividing frequency spectrum into separate bands
Channelization method dividing time into specific slots for each station
Channelization method using unique codes to allow simultaneous transmission
Frame sent to confirm successful receipt of data

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a timeline as a road. Your frame is a car. Any other car (frame) entering the road during the time your car is on the road (2T) causes a crash (collision). Both cars destroy each other.

Same road, but now there are designated parking spots (time slots). You can only enter at the start of a parking spot. This halves your vulnerability window from 2T to T, doubling your chances of success.

Picture a conference room. Before speaking, you listen (carrier sense). If someone is talking, you wait. If silent, you speak. This prevents most interruptions but collisions can still happen if two people start at exactly the same moment after hearing silence.

Imagine two people talking on a phone line simultaneously. Both hear the garbled sound (collision detection). Both immediately stop (jam signal), hang up, wait random time, then try again. The key is stopping FAST to save time.

Think of a polite wireless meeting: Person A raises hand (RTS). Host says 'Go ahead' (CTS). A speaks, others wait. After A finishes, everyone hears 'Thank you' (ACK). This prevents the 'hidden node' problem where A and B can't hear each other but both try to talk to C.

Imagine a microphone (token) passed around a meeting table. Only the person holding the microphone can speak. After speaking, they pass it to the next person. No one talks over anyone else. The token ensures orderly access.

Imagine a party where everyone speaks a different language simultaneously. You only understand your language (code), so you filter out others. All conversations happen at once (same frequency), but you're only listening to your language.

Think of a game where after each failed attempt, you choose from a doubling range of waiting times: first collision → choose from [0,1] slots; second → [0,3]; third → [0,7]; and so on. More collisions = more patience needed = less chance of repeating collision.

analogies.analogy: At a party, whenever you have something to say, just say it without checking if others are talking. If two people speak at the same time, both stop, wait random time, then try again. Inefficient but simple.
A group discussion where before speaking, you check if anyone else is currently talking. If someone is speaking, you wait for them to finish. Only when silence returns do you start talking.
A walkie-talkie conversation: When you press to talk, if you hear someone else's voice (collision), you immediately release and say 'Sorry, you go ahead.' Then wait a random time before trying again.

Asking permission at a meeting: 'May I speak?' (RTS). Host replies 'Yes, go ahead' (CTS). You speak, others stay silent. After you're done, host confirms 'Message received' (ACK). This avoids talking over someone who couldn't see you.

A roundtable discussion with a talking stick. The stick (token) circulates. Only the person holding the stick can speak. After speaking, they pass the stick to the next person. No one interrupts because only one person has the stick.
A TV schedule: Each show gets a specific time slot. MTV shows 9-10 PM, ESPN shows 10-11 PM. They never overlap; each has its dedicated time on the same channel (frequency).
Radio stations: Each station (station) gets its own frequency. FM 92.1 only plays rock, FM 104.3 only plays classical. All play simultaneously but on different frequencies, never interfering.
A crowded room with multiple conversations happening in the same language, but each pair uses a unique code word. You filter conversations by listening only for your code word, ignoring others.

Person A and Person C are far apart, can't see each other. Both see Person B in the middle. A and C both think the channel is free because they can't see each other. Both start talking to B at the same time, causing collision at B.

Person B is transmitting to A. Person C sees B is busy, so C thinks it can't send to D. But actually, C's transmission to D wouldn't interfere with B's transmission to A since they're in different directions. C unnecessarily waits.
